analogy: Think of "Diversity in Living Organisms" through the chapter's own opening images: Work, Energy, and Simple Machines Chapter 7 In earlier Chapters 4 and 6, you have learnt how forces change the motion of objects, and how kinematic equations and Newton’s laws can be used to analyse motion. But when forces change with time or act in... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 7.1 Work Done by a Constant Force, 3F F F F F, 1 J = 1 kg m s–2 × 1 m = 1 kg m2 s–2, 7.1.1 When is work done equal to zero?. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
Diversity in Living Organisms is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that work, Energy, and Simple Machines Chapter 7 In earlier Chapters 4 and 6, you have learnt how forces change the motion of objects, and how kinematic equations and Newton’s laws can be used to analyse motion.

The text explains that but when forces change with time or act in complicated ways, applying these laws directly can become difficult.

The text explains that is there a simpler and more powerful way to understand such situations?

The text explains that in this chapter, you will explore the ideas of work, energy and power, which often allow us to analyse motion and interactions more easily.

The text explains that you will also learn about simple machines, which help us perform tasks with less effort and more convenience.

The text explains that these form the building blocks of many everyday machines.

The text explains that energy, which is the capacity to do work, lies at the heart of all these ideas and of almost every activity in our daily life (Fig.

The text explains that 7.1: Energy required to carry out tasks comes from various sources Food provides energy to walk Electricity provides energy to rotate a fan Fuel provides energy to move a car We often use the words work, energy, and power in everyday conversations.

The text explains that as we learnt in Chapter 1, these terms have a precise meaning in science.

The text explains that let us first understand how to define work. y What will be the magnitude of velocity of the child at the bottom of the blue slide? y Will two children of different masses reach the bottom of the same slide with the same velocity? y Which of the slides will result in the largest magnitude of velocity for the child at its bottom?

The text explains that work, Energy, and Simple Machines 117 7.1 Work Done by a Constant Force Let us begin by doing some thinking based on our experience of lifting objects to a height.

The text explains that consider a wheat bag of mass 5 kg kept on the floor (Fig.

The text explains that gravitational force mg acts downwards on the bag, where m is the mass of the bag and g is the acceleration due to gravity.

The text explains that to lift the bag slowly to a height of 1 m, you must apply an upward force equal to mg.

The text explains that the force applied by you acts upwards on the bag as the bag is displaced through a distance of 1 m in the direction of the force.

The text explains that in everyday language, you would say that you did some work.

The text explains that the work done by a constant force acting on an object in bringing about a certain displacement can be defined as: work done on an object by a constant force = force applied × displacement in the direction of the force (7.1) In the example that we discussed, the displacement was in the vertical direction, however, Eq.

The text explains that (7.1) can be used even if the force and displacement, both are in a horizontal direction, or any other direction for that matter.

The text explains that for example, consider an object upon which a constant force F is acting, and it undergoes a displacement s in the direction of force (Fig.

The text explains that 7.3: Work done by a force while displacing an object in (a) horizontal direction, and (b) vertical direction (a) s s F While describing the work done, it is important to specify the force (or agency) doing the work and the object on which the work is done.

The text explains that (7.2), 1 joule can be defined as 1 J = 1 N × 1 m That is, 1 joule of work is done on an object when a constant force of 1 newton is applied to it and it is displaced by 1 metre in the direction of the force.

The text explains that 7.4, the force on an object is plotted on the Y-axis against the displacement in the direction of force on the X-axis.

The text explains that if a force acts in a direction perpendicular to the displacement of an object, the work done by that force is zero (Fig.

The text explains that 7.6) because there is no displacement in the direction of the force.

The text explains that 7.6: Carrying a box Displacement Next Level Up 7.1.2 Positive and negative work done The work done by a force on an object can either be positive or negative depending upon the relative directions of the force and the displacement.

The text explains that when the displacement is in the same direction as the applied force, the work done by the force on the object is said to be positive.

The text explains that 7.5: Pushing a wall Force and displacement have both magnitude and direction.

The text explains that work, Energy, and Simple Machines 119 when you push a wheelchair, the force applied by you on the wheelchair and its displacement are in the same direction (Fig.

The text explains that when the displacement is in the direction opposite to that of the force, then the work done by the force on the object is negative.

The text explains that for example, while stopping a ball, the goalkeeper applies a force in a direction opposite to the direction of motion of the football (Fig.

The text explains that when she moves the dumbbell up, the force is in the direction of displacement, so she does positive work on it.

The text explains that when moving the dumbbell down, the force she applies to hold it is in a direction opposite to the displacement, so she does negative work on it.

The text explains that the displacement should be taken as negative because the ball moves in a direction opposite to the direction of the applied force.

The text explains that work done by the goalkeeper on the ball = force × displacement of ball in the direction of force = 200 N × (– 0.15 m) = – 30 J 1.

The text explains that in the previous chapter, a weightlifter is shown holding a barbell steady in her hands (Fig.

The text explains that the relation between the work done on an object and the change in its energy is called the work-energy theorem, which can be stated as work done on an object = change in its energy (7.3) This theorem also holds for a system of objects or even when the forces applied on an object are not constant.

The text explains that when two objects at different temperatures come in contact, energy flows from the hotter one to the colder one.

The text explains that for example, the Sun’s energy reaches the Earth through radiation.

The text explains that energy is transferred in electric circuits, as well as via sound waves, and even in nuclear reactions that power the Sun.

The text explains that the moving striker applies force in the direction of displacement of the white coin.

The text explains that among these, we will now look more closely at mechanical energy, since it is directly connected to the forces and motions you studied earlier.

The text explains that let v be its Grade 7 Curiosity Chapter 3 Grade 6 Curiosity Chapter 3 Mechanical energy Energy due to motion or position of objects Energy related to position or motion of charges Thermal energy Energy that makes things warm or hot Light energy Energy that allows us to see Sound energy Energy of vibrations of air or other molecules Energy stored in the nuclei of atoms Energy stored in fuels and food in the form of chemical bonds between atoms Nuclear energy Chemical energy Electrical energy Fig.

The text explains that the displacement of the aircraft and the force that the wire applies are in opposite directions.

The text explains that pause and Ponder 7.4.2 Potential energy In Chapter 6, How Forces Affect Motion, you performed an activity (6.1) where you could move a stack of coins by releasing the stretched rubber band with which it was in contact.

The text explains that in this case, when you release the stretched elastic band, the object in contact with it shoots in the forward direction (Fig.

The text explains that the archer pulls on the string of the bow bending its arms in the process.

The text explains that the refill shoots out, showing the conversion of elastic potential energy into kinetic energy.

The text explains that repeat with different amounts of stretch, and observe how the distance travelled changes.

The text explains that is there a relationship between the stretch and the distance travelled? y Construct one or more simple machines, or a combination of them (lever, pulley, and inclined plane) using easily available materials, such as cardboard, wooden strips or rulers, pencils or bolts (to act as a fulcrum), thread or rope, small pulleys (or two bottle caps stuck together), and paper cups to hold small weights.

The text explains that use your model to lift or move a small load, measure the effort and the load, and calculate the mechanical advantage. y Computer simulations can help in visualising physical quantities that are difficult to observe directly.

The text explains that the PhET simulations (https:// phet.colorado.edu) provide interactive models, such as Energy Skate Park, Energy Forms and Changes, Pendulum Lab, and Masses and Springs.

The text explains that use these to explore how different forms of energy change as parameters, such as mass, height, and friction are varied.

Source: NCERT Class 9 Science, Chapter 7: Diversity in Living Organisms

Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
